annotations.label: Partial differential equation (PDE)

anchor.snippet: a partial differential equation ( PDE ) is an equation which involves a multivariable function and one or more of its partial derivatives

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general PDE abstraction; individual equations (e.g. Laplace) appear only ad hoc via the Laplacian operator.

annotations.label: Harmonic function

anchor.snippet: A function that obeys this equation is called a harmonic function

annotations.note: `HarmonicAt` defines harmonicity via vanishing Laplacian on real finite-dimensional inner product spaces, generalizing the planar definition.

annotations.label: Harmonic functions of various forms

anchor.snippet: It may be surprising that these examples of harmonic functions are of such different forms

annotations.note: The specific catalogue of example harmonic functions (e^x cos y, log(x²+y²), etc.) is not present as named declarations.

annotations.label: Solutions v(x,y) = f(x) + g(y)

anchor.snippet: It can be directly checked that any function v of the form

annotations.note: No declaration formalizes that f(x)+g(y) solves v_{xy}=0 on the plane.

annotations.label: Existence/uniqueness on the unit disk

anchor.snippet: Let B denote the unit-radius disk around the origin in the plane

annotations.note: The Dirichlet problem on the disk is not stated; the Poisson formula in `Harmonic/Poisson.lean` gives a representation but no existence/uniqueness theorem.

annotations.label: Existence/uniqueness on the strip

anchor.snippet: For any functions f and g on the real line

annotations.note: No existence/uniqueness result for v_{xy}=0 on a strip with prescribed boundary data is present.

annotations.label: Affine solutions from differential-geometric PDE

anchor.snippet: then there are numbers a , b , and c with

annotations.note: The Monge–Ampère-type rigidity statement classifying affine solutions is not present.

annotations.label: Linear PDE

anchor.snippet: A linear PDE is one such that, if it is homogeneous, the sum of any two solutions is also a solution

annotations.note: There is no abstraction of linear PDE in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Partial differential equation (formal)

anchor.snippet: A partial differential equation is an equation that involves an unknown function of

annotations.note: No formal data type for PDEs (relation among an unknown function and its partials) is defined.

annotations.label: Laplace operator

anchor.snippet: The Greek letter Δ denotes the Laplace operator

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Δ via `Laplacian.laplacian` (instance for inner-product spaces), with the standard orthonormal-basis formula.

annotations.label: Linear PDE

anchor.snippet: A PDE is called linear if it is linear in the unknown and its derivatives

annotations.note: No general definition of a linear PDE exists.

annotations.label: Linear with constant coefficients

anchor.snippet: then the PDE is called linear with constant coefficients

annotations.note: No notion of constant-coefficient linear PDE is formalized.

annotations.label: Homogeneous / inhomogeneous linear PDE

anchor.snippet: If f is zero everywhere then the linear PDE is homogeneous , otherwise it is inhomogeneous

annotations.note: Not formalized — there is no PDE type to be homogeneous over.

annotations.label: Semi-linear PDE

anchor.snippet: Nearest to linear PDEs are semi-linear PDEs, where only the highest order derivatives appear as linear terms

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quasilinear PDE

anchor.snippet: In a quasilinear PDE the highest order derivatives likewise appear only as linear terms

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fully nonlinear PDE

anchor.snippet: A PDE without any linearity properties is called fully nonlinear

annotations.note: Not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic PDE (discriminant)

anchor.snippet: Solutions of elliptic PDEs are as smooth as the coefficients allow

annotations.note: Mathlib's `elliptic` occurrences refer to elliptic curves, not PDE classification by discriminant B²−AC.

annotations.label: Parabolic PDE (discriminant)

anchor.snippet: Equations that are parabolic at every point can be transformed into a form analogous to the heat equation

annotations.note: No PDE-discriminant classification is present.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic PDE (discriminant)

anchor.snippet: hyperbolic equations retain any discontinuities of functions or derivatives in the initial data

annotations.note: No PDE-discriminant classification is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic classification by eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: Elliptic: the eigenvalues are all positive or all negative

annotations.note: Higher-dimensional eigenvalue classification of second-order operators is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parabolic classification by eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: Parabolic: the eigenvalues are all positive or all negative, except one that is zero

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic classification by eigenvalues

anchor.snippet: Hyperbolic: there is only one negative eigenvalue and all the rest are positive

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Ultrahyperbolic classification

anchor.snippet: Ultrahyperbolic: there is more than one positive eigenvalue and more than one negative eigenvalue

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Characteristic surface

anchor.section: Systems of first-order equations and characteristic surfaces
anchor.snippet: then S is a characteristic surface for the operator L at a given point if the characteristic form vanishes

annotations.note: Characteristic surfaces of PDE operators are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic first-order system

anchor.section: Systems of first-order equations and characteristic surfaces
anchor.snippet: A first-order system Lu = 0 is elliptic if no surface is characteristic for L

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Hyperbolic first-order system

anchor.section: Systems of first-order equations and characteristic surfaces
anchor.snippet: A first-order system is hyperbolic at a point if there is a spacelike surface S with normal

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Strictly hyperbolic system

anchor.section: Systems of first-order equations and characteristic surfaces
anchor.snippet: The system is strictly hyperbolic if these roots are always distinct

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Separable partial differential equation

anchor.snippet: This is possible for simple PDEs, which are called separable partial differential equations

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize separation of variables or separable PDEs.

annotations.label: Characteristic curve

anchor.snippet: The characteristic surface in n = 2 - dimensional space is called a characteristic curve

annotations.note: Not formalized.

annotations.label: Method of characteristics

anchor.snippet: changing coordinates in the domain to straighten these curves allows separation of variables, and is called the method of characteristics

annotations.note: The method of characteristics is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Superposition principle for linear PDEs

anchor.snippet: are solutions of linear PDE in some function space

annotations.note: A general superposition principle for linear PDEs is not stated; only special instances such as `HarmonicAt.add` / `const_smul` for the Laplace equation are present.

annotations.label: Weak solution

anchor.snippet: Weak solutions are functions that satisfy the PDE, yet in other meanings than regular sense

annotations.note: Mathlib has distributions and Sobolev spaces (`Mathlib.Analysis.Distribution.Sobolev`) but no notion of `weak solution` of a PDE.

annotations.label: Weak solution example

anchor.snippet: An example for a weak solution is as follows

annotations.note: The illustrative weak-solution example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Well-posedness

anchor.snippet: Well-posedness refers to a common schematic package of information about a PDE

annotations.note: No Hadamard well-posedness predicate for PDEs exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regularity

anchor.snippet: Regularity refers to the integrability and differentiability of weak solutions

annotations.note: Mathlib's many `Regular*` declarations are unrelated (topology, measure theory, etc.); PDE regularity of weak solutions is not formalized.
